name: fabled ancient child statue
shortDescription: A weather-smoothed statue of a child from an age that left more questions than roads. Its small hands are folded around a vanished offering, and collectors argue whether it was made for remembrance, protection, or a rite no living temple will admit to knowing.
flavorText: “The face is young. The stone is older than any kingdom that claims it.”

name: fabled ancient wolf statue
shortDescription: A heavy wolf statue carved with old pack marks, its muzzle lifted as if listening to a moon no one else can see. Hunters prize it as a ward, nobles as a trophy, and scholars as a clue to border cults that respected wolves more than kings.
flavorText: “Some statues guard doors. This one seems to guard a memory of the wild.”

name: fabled ancient woman statue
shortDescription: An ancient woman statue with calm eyes, layered robes, and hands worn smooth by generations of touch. It carries the dignity of a forgotten matriarch, saint, or judge, depending on which buyer is trying to raise or lower the price.
flavorText: “Every village claims she blessed their well. None agree on her name.”

name: fabled golden dog statue
shortDescription: A massive golden dog statue, too grand for a kennel and too watchful for ordinary decoration. Its polished ears and paws show where owners rubbed it for luck before voyages, trials, and negotiations that needed loyal witnesses.
flavorText: “Gold makes it valuable. The faithful stare makes it difficult to sell.”

name: large barrel
shortDescription: A large wooden barrel built for cellars, ships, and warehouses where volume matters more than elegance. The staves are broad, the hoops sit tight, and the inside smells faintly of grain, brine, or whatever last merchant forgot to label it.
flavorText: “A barrel is only boring until it leaks in the wrong hold.”

name: massive barrel
shortDescription: A massive barrel made for serious bulk trade, the sort rolled by teams and cursed by dockhands. It can hold drink, oil, salted goods, or one regrettable accounting mistake large enough to trouble an entire guild.
flavorText: “No honest merchant buys one unless they plan to fill it.”

name: medium barrel
shortDescription: A sturdy medium barrel, easier to move than the cellar giants but large enough for profitable trade. It suits taverns, caravans, kitchens, and cautious wholesalers who would rather lose one barrel than a whole season’s stock.
flavorText: “The favorite size of people who have learned not to trust roads.”

name: small barrel
shortDescription: A small barrel with tight hoops and a practical shape, often used for samples, strong drink, pickled goods, or private reserves hidden behind larger cargo. Buyers like it because one person can lift it and two can argue over it.
flavorText: “Small barrels travel quickly and disappear even faster.”

name: large basket
shortDescription: A large woven basket built for harvest tables, laundry rooms, market stalls, and anything that needs to breathe. Its reeds are thick enough for rough handling, though a wise merchant still checks the bottom before loading onions.
flavorText: “A basket proves civilization began when someone got tired of dropping things.”

name: medium basket
shortDescription: A medium basket with a steady weave and a useful carry size, common in kitchens, gardens, and day markets. It is not glamorous stock, but every household finds a reason to buy another before the old one has quite failed.
flavorText: “The humble basket always sells because hands are never enough.”

name: small basket
shortDescription: A small basket suited for eggs, herbs, berries, loose tools, or the tiny purchases that make market days feel successful. Its value comes from convenience, and convenience is one of the few luxuries even poor buyers understand.
flavorText: “Not everything worth carrying needs a chest.”

name: steel cage
shortDescription: A steel cage with reinforced bars, heavy hinges, and a lock plate meant to survive panic, weather, and bad planning. Traders use it for rare animals, dangerous cargo, or valuable things they would rather not explain in detail.
flavorText: “A cage says less about what is inside than about how badly someone wants it kept there.”

name: wooden cage
shortDescription: A wooden cage made from stout slats and pegged joints, lighter than steel and easier to repair on the road. It serves animal traders, poultry sellers, and anyone moving noisy cargo that should arrive alive but not necessarily pleased.
flavorText: “Cheap cages rattle. Good cages only complain when opened.”

name: handcart
shortDescription: A plain handcart with worn handles, a strong bed, and wheels that have known cobbles, mud, and warehouse floors. It turns one tired back into a small trading operation, which makes it more valuable than its humble shape suggests.
flavorText: “The first empire was probably built by someone with a cart and a sore shoulder.”

name: horse wagon
shortDescription: A horse wagon built for road trade, with a broad bed, high sides, and enough old dents to prove it has earned its keep. It carries harvests, crates, families, rumors, and occasionally merchants who overestimated their stamina.
flavorText: “A wagon is a shop that agreed to move.”

name: fancy horse carriage
shortDescription: A fancy horse carriage with polished trim, cushioned seating, and the kind of suspension that announces money before the passenger speaks. Nobles hire it for appearances, merchants rent it for negotiations, and drivers judge everyone silently.
flavorText: “Comfort costs extra because discomfort has no shortage of customers.”

name: scarce ornate blue chest
shortDescription: A scarce ornate blue chest with enamel panels, bright fittings, and a lock that looks more ceremonial than simple. Its color makes it popular for dowries and guild gifts, while its weight reminds thieves that beauty can be inconvenient.
flavorText: “A chest this blue is not hiding wealth. It is daring the room to notice.”

name: scarce ornate red chest
shortDescription: A scarce ornate red chest lacquered like a festival lantern and trimmed for display. It is bought for jewels, contracts, wedding linens, or anything the owner wants guests to imagine is more valuable than it may actually be.
flavorText: “Red chests make secrets look expensive.”

name: scarce puzzle chest
shortDescription: A scarce puzzle chest fitted with sliding panels, false seams, and a lock that enjoys embarrassing impatient owners. It attracts collectors, smugglers, and puzzle-minded heirs who believe clever woodwork can protect foolish fortunes.
flavorText: “The key is usually obvious after someone else opens it.”

name: steel chest
shortDescription: A steel chest with blunt corners, heavy plates, and very little interest in elegance. It is the practical answer to dangerous roads, dishonest servants, and merchants who sleep better when their coin is harder to carry away.
flavorText: “Not pretty, not light, not easy to ignore.”

name: fabled magic chest
shortDescription: A fabled magic chest that hums faintly near old spells and refuses to feel completely empty. The hinges move with suspicious grace, and buyers often ask whether it protects treasure, hides treasure, or has opinions about treasure.
flavorText: “A normal chest stores valuables. This one seems to evaluate them.”

name: wooden chest
shortDescription: A wooden chest of the reliable sort found at the foot of beds, in wagon beds, and behind counters. Its boards are plain but honest, and with a better lock it can become almost respectable cargo.
flavorText: “Every household has one chest that knows too much.”

name: fabled solid gold chest
shortDescription: A fabled solid gold chest so heavy that owning it becomes a logistics problem before it becomes a pleasure. The workmanship is magnificent, the shine is ridiculous, and its safest use may be convincing visitors not to ask what is inside.
flavorText: “When the container is treasure, the contents must work hard to impress.”

name: large strong chest
shortDescription: A large strong chest reinforced for weight, weather, and rough handling. It is meant for serious stores: coin bags, ledgers, silverware, sealed letters, or the kind of heirloom that starts family arguments the moment it appears.
flavorText: “Strength is a virtue in furniture and a warning in inheritance.”

name: large wooen chest
shortDescription: A large wooen chest, plainly named and sturdily made, with broad boards that invite sanding, staining, and a better lock. Its misshapen label has not hurt sales; practical buyers care more about whether the lid closes true.
flavorText: “A scribe can misspell wood. A carpenter cannot.”

name: large crate
shortDescription: A large crate built for bulk movement, nailed firm and scarred by hooks, ropes, and impatient handlers. It is not meant to be loved, only stacked, counted, shipped, and opened without discovering half the goods have become splinters.
flavorText: “Crates are the punctuation marks of trade routes.”

name: medium crate
shortDescription: A medium crate sized for manageable cargo and repeated use, common in warehouses where every worker has opinions about weight. It suits tools, pottery, dried goods, and anything valuable enough to keep together but not enough for a chest.
flavorText: “The best crates are remembered only when they fail.”

name: small crate
shortDescription: A small crate with tight corners and a liftable shape, useful for samples, fragile sets, or parts that vanish if left loose. It is cheap stock, but merchants like cheap things that prevent expensive mistakes.
flavorText: “Small crates keep small losses from becoming big ones.”

name: backpack
shortDescription: A travel-worn backpack with enough straps, pockets, and patched seams to suggest a life outdoors. It appeals to couriers, pilgrims, students, and adventurers who think everything important should fit on their shoulders.
flavorText: “A backpack is a private warehouse with sore straps.”

name: belt bag
shortDescription: A belt bag made for coins, herbs, keys, and the small items a traveler needs before stopping. It sits close to the body, which makes it beloved by cautious merchants and disliked by nimble-fingered market thieves.
flavorText: “The best place for coin is where your elbow can argue for it.”

name: sling bag
shortDescription: A sling bag with a quick throw-on strap and enough room for daily trade goods, letters, lunch, or one suspiciously heavy parcel. It is favored by messengers who need both hands free and excuses ready.
flavorText: “Easy to carry, easier to overfill.”

name: black pot
shortDescription: A black pot darkened by firing, soot, and use, broad enough for practical cooking yet handsome enough for a shelf. It sells to kitchens, camps, and potion-makers who insist the color improves the mood of boiling things.
flavorText: “Every cook owns a pot that looks guilty.”

name: black pot cracked
shortDescription: A cracked black pot with a visible fracture that turns it from kitchenware into cheap storage, planter, prop, or warning. Some buyers call it damaged; others call it character and ask for a discount anyway.
flavorText: “A crack is just a story that lowered the price.”

name: large brown pot
shortDescription: A large brown pot with a rounded belly and a steady base, useful for grains, pickles, water, or the household mysteries kept away from children. Its earthy glaze makes it common, reliable, and hard to keep in stock.
flavorText: “Brown pots do not boast. They endure.”

name: medium brown pot
shortDescription: A medium brown pot of the everyday sort that moves from market to kitchen without ceremony. It is sized for usefulness rather than grandeur, perfect for cooks who need storage before they need compliments.
flavorText: “The pot nobody praises is usually the one everyone borrows.”

name: pottery black
shortDescription: A piece of black pottery with a clean silhouette and a dark fired finish that suits both practical shelves and dramatic rooms. It looks more expensive by candlelight, a fact merchants are happy to demonstrate near closing time.
flavorText: “Some clay learns elegance from smoke.”

name: pottery blue
shortDescription: A blue pottery vessel glazed with cheerful color and a merchant-friendly shine. It is bought for shelves, tables, dowries, and inns that want guests to believe the water jug has artistic ambitions.
flavorText: “Blue glaze makes ordinary clay remember the sky.”

name: pottery brown
shortDescription: A brown pottery vessel with plain curves and useful weight, the sort that blends into kitchens until someone needs it urgently. Its strength is not beauty but trust; it looks like it belongs anywhere practical work happens.
flavorText: “Honest clay, honest price, honest chance of being chipped by summer.”

name: pottery deep brown
shortDescription: A deep brown pottery piece with a richer glaze than common kitchenware, warm as roasted chestnuts and dark as old beams. It sits comfortably between humble storage and respectable display, which broadens its list of buyers.
flavorText: “The color of earth after rain always sells better than plain mud.”

name: large ornate blue yellow pottery
shortDescription: A large ornate blue yellow pottery vessel painted for attention, with bright bands and curling details that make it unsuitable for quiet corners. It is heavy, showy, and popular with hosts who believe storage should impress guests.
flavorText: “Some pots hold grain. This one holds a room’s attention.”

name: pottery ornate brown
shortDescription: An ornate brown pottery piece carved and glazed with enough care to rise above pantry duty. Its decoration feels old-fashioned in the profitable way, suggesting family recipes, respectable cupboards, and inherited taste.
flavorText: “A plain pot serves supper. An ornate one claims the family always had manners.”

name: pottery ornate red yellow
shortDescription: A red and yellow ornate pottery vessel with festival colors and a bold market presence. It is bought for celebration tables, public houses, and anyone who wants a shelf to look warmer before the fire is lit.
flavorText: “It looks full of summer even when empty.”

name: pottery ornate white blue
shortDescription: An ornate white blue pottery piece with clean glaze, neat patterning, and a coastal feel. It appeals to refined households and seaside inns, especially when a seller mentions distant kilns whether or not they were involved.
flavorText: “Blue on white makes every room pretend it has a sea breeze.”

name: pottery skinny brown
shortDescription: A skinny brown pottery vessel made for narrow shelves, oils, stems, or small portions that should pour slowly. Its slim shape gives it elegance without losing the grounded charm of ordinary fired clay.
flavorText: “A narrow pot is what happens when clay learns patience.”

name: pottery skinny orange
shortDescription: A skinny orange pottery vessel with a warm glaze that catches torchlight and tavern windows. It is too bright to vanish into a cupboard, which makes it useful for display, sauces, and anyone decorating on a budget.
flavorText: “Orange clay never whispers.”

name: pottery skinny white
shortDescription: A skinny white pottery piece with a pale glaze and a clean, careful shape. It is popular among healers, scribes, and fussy households that like their shelves orderly and their vessels easy to inspect for stains.
flavorText: “White pottery makes dirt confess quickly.”

name: small brown pottery
shortDescription: A small brown pottery item suited to spices, ink, table salt, buttons, or whatever tiny thing a household swears it will organize this time. It is modest, useful, and almost always bought in pairs after the first one proves handy.
flavorText: “Small vessels are where clutter goes to look intentional.”

name: small ornate blue yellow pottery
shortDescription: A small ornate blue yellow pottery piece, bright enough for a gift and useful enough to escape pure decoration. Its painted bands make it popular with travelers who want something pretty that will still fit in a pack.
flavorText: “A little color travels better than a large apology.”

name: pottery white
shortDescription: A white pottery vessel with a smooth glaze and a plain confident form. It suits clean tables, apothecary shelves, and wealthy kitchens where even storage is expected to behave politely.
flavorText: “White clay looks innocent until the first stew.”

name: pottery yellow
shortDescription: A yellow pottery vessel with a sunny glaze that makes even dry goods look cheerful. It sells well in spring markets, wedding bundles, and gloomy towns where people claim they are buying practicality, not hope.
flavorText: “A yellow pot is cheaper than better weather.”
